references.title: Early prediction of medical students’ performance in high-stakes examinations using machine learning approaches

extracted.Data sources: Demographic Information, Entrance and Admission Records, Grade History
extracted.Features:
[Birthday, Gender, Residency Status, Registration Date, Entrance Semester, Type of Admission, Age at Entrance, Grade Anatomical Sciences, Grade Biochemistry, Grade Bacteriology, Grade Epidemiology, Grade General Knowledge, Grade Mycology, 
GPA, Previous Attempts, Exam Year, Age at Exam]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Students who had records but did not follow through to the CMBSEwere excluded from the sample. 

The features were extracted from the CES data. Data was normalized and the selection of features was done with the recourse to SHAP. Authors evaluate multple oversampling techniques to address class imbalance (ROS, SMOTE, RUS, Tomek and SMOTE TOMEK), with SMOTE Tomek being selected as the “final” option.
extracted.Models: Logistic Regression, kNN, SVM, Bagging, Random Forest, AdaBoost, XGB, Stacking, Voting
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {AUC: 0.813 - RF, Precision-Recall: 0.93 - RF, PPV: 0.871 - RF, Recall: 0.828 - Stacking, Accuracy: 0.829 - Stacking, F1-Score: 0.837 - Stacking, MCC: 0.515 - RF, BS - 0.121 - Bagging}
extracted.Comments:
Authors present a set of models to predict performance on a test. 

Authors do a standard ML pipeline with Feature Selection being done with SHAP. 

The models are evaluated using multiple metrics and the Ensemble methods achieved the best performance.
extracted.Limitations:
- Requreiires generalization to other contexts (even within medical education)

- Importance of different courses needs to be adjusted.

- No access to admission records.

- Requires validation in bigger contexts.

annotations.text: The authors propose a model whose intention is to predict how well a student will perform in an high-stakes exam. Authors make a comparison between classical ML models and Ensemble-based models.

Models are assessed on a dataset of medical students collected over a period of 5 years (1005 students). Ensemble models obtain better results at predicting the status on CMBSE exam (Regression Task). Moreover, the GPA on different important disciplines taken throughout the course exhibit high correlations with the outcomes.

The authors conclude that their approach is a good predictor of success in an important medical licencing examination AND, for that reason, the employment of these models may be considered as a viable alternative to taking the outright.
